---
title: "Why test?"
subtitle: "Testing training — Module 01"
lang: en
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    code-block-height: 400px
---

## 🇫🇷

[🇫🇷 Version française](../../slides/01_intro.qmd)

---

## A pipeline can succeed… and produce wrong output

The pipeline runs. Files are generated. Results look consistent.

. . .

**How do you know the output is correct?**

::: {.notes}
Let the question hang for 5–10 seconds. Don't answer it. If someone says "by checking manually", nod and follow up: "with 10,000 hits, is that feasible?"

The goal of this opening slide is discomfort — not information. Everyone in the room has run a pipeline and assumed the results were correct. This is the moment to make that assumption explicit.
:::

---

## Real case — KEGG / KOfam annotation

[anvi'o blog 2022](https://anvio.org/blog/bioinformatics-bugs/)

- Two tools annotate the same genes with the same KOfam database
- Expected: limited differences, explainable by method
- Observed: up to **65 % false positives**

. . .

The pipeline ran. Files were generated. **No visible error.**

::: {.notes}
The two real bugs:
1. The bit score threshold for the first KO in a batch was applied to all KOs in that batch — a subtle loop error.
2. Numeric scores were compared as strings: "9" > "80" is true as a string.

Neither bug raises an exception. The pipeline exits normally. Without a test targeting the business rule, they are undetectable.

Don't dwell on the technical details — the blog post is there for anyone who wants to go deeper. The point is: the pipeline ran, files were produced, everything looked fine. That's what makes this kind of bug dangerous.
:::

---

## The minimal test: protecting the business rule

**Bug 1 — filtering with the wrong bit score threshold**

Rule: a hit passes the filter only if its score ≥ the model's threshold.

```
KO_A  threshold = 100,  score = 120  → kept      ✓
KO_B  threshold = 500,  score =  80  → rejected  ✗
```

. . .

```python
# Weak test
assert os.path.exists("output.filtered")

# Correct test — protects the business rule
assert all(hit.score >= threshold[hit.ko] for hit in kept_hits)
```

::: {.notes}
The weak test is the most common pattern. It verifies that a file was created — not that its content is correct. It would pass even if the filter kept everything or rejected everything.

"Business rule" = the expected behaviour according to the scientific specification. Here: each hit is filtered against its own model's threshold. Simple rule, but invisible without a test.

Useful question for the group: "In your pipelines, do your tests check the file, or the content?"
:::

---

## The tools {.smaller}

| Language | Framework |
|----------|-----------|
| Nextflow | [nf-test](https://www.nf-test.com/) |
| Bash | [bats](https://bats-core.readthedocs.io/) |
| Python | [pytest](https://docs.pytest.org/) |
| R | [testthat](https://testthat.r-lib.org/) |

::: {.notes}
Don't go into detail here — each tool is covered in module 03 (bats, pytest, testthat) and module 04 (nf-test).

Common question: "Why not Python for everything?" — bats is natural for testing shell commands. pytest for Python logic. testthat for R. nf-test for the Nextflow ecosystem. Each framework is idiomatic in its context.
:::

---

## Team rule

::: {.callout-important}
**No merge without a test.**

Every bug found → a test that would have caught it.

Every Nextflow module → a `.nf.test` file.
:::

::: {.notes}
Don't present this as an imposed constraint — present it as a collective decision born from painful experience.

If someone pushes back, ask: "Who has ever merged something that broke silently?" The rule comes exactly from that.

"An empty but present `.nf.test` file" is a visible reminder in the repo that the test still needs to be written. It's a signal, not a trick.
:::

---

## Training overview {.smaller}

| Module | Topic | Exercise |
|--------|-------|----------|
| 01 | Why test | `01_bash/` |
| 02 | Concepts | — |
| 03 | Bash · Python · R | `01_bash/` `02_python/` `03_r/` |
| 04 | nf-test | `04_nextflow/` |
| 06 | Bioinformatics testing | `06_metamorphic/` |
| 07 | Team strategy | — |
| Bonus | Containers | `05_testing_containers/` |

::: {.notes}
The gap between 04 and 06 is intentional: module 05 (Apptainer containers) is a bonus. It requires Apptainer installed and a `.sif` image available — that's not always the case. Depending on the team's environment, it can be covered in-session or done independently.

Don't spend more than 30 seconds on this table — it's a high-level overview, not a session plan to comment line by line.
:::

---

## Exercise → Module 01

The goal of this exercise is to protect exactly this kind of business rule — exit code, error message, behaviour on invalid input — using bats.

Go to `exercises/01_bash/`

Deliverables: 1 happy path test · 1 empty file test · 1 missing file test · 1 assertion on the output message

::: {.notes}
The exercise is intentionally simple. `validate_records.sh` is roughly 20 lines. The goal is to practise the GIVEN/WHEN/THEN structure, not to understand the script's logic.

If someone gets stuck on bats syntax → point them to module 02 (slide "Next → Module 03") which shows the GIVEN/WHEN/THEN-to-bats translation.
:::
